title: 5 Tips for Renovating a Historic Home
author: Calvin Berry
excerpt: Renovating a historic home is rewarding but comes with unique challenges. Here are five lessons from decades of restoring older homes in North Texas.
content:
## 1. Expect the Unexpected

Older homes always have surprises behind the walls — outdated wiring, plumbing that doesn't meet code, structural issues hidden by decades of cosmetic fixes. Budget an extra 15–20% beyond your initial estimate for unknowns. It's not pessimism — it's experience.

In our 50 years of renovation work, we've never opened up a wall in a pre-1960s home without finding at least one thing that needed attention.

## 2. Preserve What Makes It Special

The character of a historic home is its greatest asset. Original hardwood floors, crown molding, built-in cabinetry, and architectural details are often irreplaceable. Before ripping anything out, take time to assess what can be restored rather than replaced.

We've refinished original heart pine floors that were hidden under carpet for 40 years. The results are always stunning and add real value to the home.

## 3. Upgrade Systems, Not Just Surfaces

The most important investments in an older home are the ones you can't see:

- **Electrical**: Knob-and-tube or aluminum wiring needs to be replaced for safety
- **Plumbing**: Galvanized pipes corrode from the inside and should be upgraded to copper or PEX
- **Insulation**: Most pre-1970s homes have inadequate insulation — upgrading dramatically improves comfort and energy bills
- **HVAC**: Modern systems are far more efficient and can be installed without destroying historic details

## 4. Work with Experienced Professionals

Renovating a historic home requires different skills than new construction. Look for contractors who:

- Have specific experience with older homes
- Understand how to work with plaster, lathe, and older framing techniques
- Know how to match existing trim profiles and architectural details
- Respect the home's character while making practical improvements

A contractor who's great at building new homes may not be the right fit for a careful restoration.

## 5. Check Local Historic Regulations

If your home is in a designated historic district, you may need approval from a local historic preservation board before making exterior changes. Even some interior work may have guidelines. Check with your city's planning department early in the process to avoid costly delays.

In some cases, historic designation qualifies you for tax credits or grants that can offset renovation costs — it's worth investigating.

## The Reward Is Worth the Effort

There's nothing quite like living in a beautifully restored historic home. The craftsmanship in older homes — solid wood framing, plaster walls, handmade details — simply isn't replicated in modern construction. With the right approach and the right team, you can enjoy all that character with modern comfort.

Thinking about renovating your older home? [Contact us](/contact.html) to discuss your project — we love bringing historic homes back to life.
